coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent sensor based on an enzyme-free system for highly selective and sensitive detection of glyphosate and malathion in real samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Aydin, Ziya
Keskinateş, Mukaddes
Akın, Şeyma
Keleş, Hülya
Keleş, Mustafa
coredata.dc:description: Malathion (Mal) and glyphosate (Glyp) are widely used organophosphorus pesticides to prevent or control insects and weed in agriculture. Unfortunately, the extensive and frequent commercial use of these compounds on farmland has caused their presence as residues in crops and livestock. Therefore, the design of new sensors with enhanced analyte selectivity and sensitivity is critical in this field. Here, we developed an optical sensor for the detection of malathion and glyphosate. The sensor, N,N-dimethyl-4-(3-(pyrazin-2-ylimino)prop-1-en-1-yl)aniline (CNP), was synthesized in a one-step reaction by condensation of 4-(dimethylamino)cinnamaldehyde with 2-aminopyrazine. CNP gives a rapid color change, absorbance and fluorescence response to Glyp and Mal in the presence of the metal ions and pesticides tested in the working system. The binding stoichiometry between the sensor and the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) was investigated by some spectrophotometric methods and the sensing mechanisms were eludicated by NMR titration experiments, suggesting that CNP responds to Glyp via H-bonding and to Mal via the coordination. The sensor has a high selectivity and rapid response to the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) with low detection limits. The sensor was also successfully used to monitor the pesticides in real samples; Mal in apple and tap water samples, and Glyp in potato, tap water and soil samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cinnamaldehyde
Colorimetric
Optical sensor
Glyphosate
Malathion
Pesticide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the sensor, CNP
Determination of the pesticides (Glyp, Mal) in real samples
Spectroscopic studies of the sensor, CNP
Application of the sensor, CNP, in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK, Project number: 219Z289) for the financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Changes in (a) Absorption and (b) Fluorescence intensity of CNP (100 μM) after addition of the pesticides (Mal, Glyp, Fena, Par (100 µM each)) and metal ions (Cu2+ , Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Na+ and Al3+ (100 μM)) in ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v).

The fluorescence intensities of CNP (100 µM) with Glyp/Mal (100 µM) with/without different pesticide/metal ions (100 µM) in ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v). (The bars represent the fluorescence intensity at 595 nm.).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CNP with the gradual addition of of Glyp (0, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150 µM). (b) Fluorescence spectra of CNP with the addition of different concentrations of Mal (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150 µM).
1H NMR spectra of CNP (black line) and CNP + Glyp (blue line).
1H NMR spectra of CNP (black line) and CNP + Mal (blue line).
Colorimetric changes in CNP (100 μM) after addition of different concentrations of Glyp. Left to right: 0, 2, 4, 8, 16, and 20 μM.
Proposed mechanism between the sensor, CNP, and Glyp.
Proposed mechanism between the sensor, CNP, and Mal.
Determination of malathion and glyphosate in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: [CNP] 100 µM in ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v).

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent sensor based on an enzyme-free system for highly selective and sensitive detection of glyphosate and malathion in real samples

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Vocational School of Technical Sciences, Karamanoğlu Mehmetbey University, 70100 Karaman, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Malathion (Mal) and glyphosate (Glyp) are widely used organophosphorus pesticides to prevent or control insects and weed in agriculture. Unfortunately, the extensive and frequent commercial use of these compounds on farmland has caused their presence as residues in crops and livestock. Therefore, the design of new sensors with enhanced analyte selectivity and sensitivity is critical in this field. Here, we developed an optical sensor for the detection of malathion and glyphosate. The sensor, N,N-dimethyl-4-(3-(pyrazin-2-ylimino)prop-1-en-1-yl)aniline (CNP), was synthesized in a one-step reaction by condensation of 4-(dimethylamino)cinnamaldehyde with 2-aminopyrazine. CNP gives a rapid color change, absorbance and fluorescence response to Glyp and Mal in the presence of the metal ions and pesticides tested in the working system. The binding stoichiometry between the sensor and the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) was investigated by some spectrophotometric methods and the sensing mechanisms were eludicated by NMR titration experiments, suggesting that CNP responds to Glyp via H-bonding and to Mal via the coordination. The sensor has a high selectivity and rapid response to the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) with low detection limits. The sensor was also successfully used to monitor the pesticides in real samples; Mal in apple and tap water samples, and Glyp in potato, tap water and soil samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus (OP) pesticides have been widely used around the world to protect vegetables and crops from pest and pest hazard because they are highly efficient, persistent and broad spectrum [1–3]. Mal and Glyp are among OP pesticides. Mal was first registered as an insecticide in 1956 and, it has been among the best-selling OP insecticide since the 1980s [4]. On the other hand, Glyp, which is a broad-spectrum herbicide, is extensively used worldwide because it has a relatively low toxicity to mammals and it has high herbicide performance [5]. Hence, their wide uses have caused a large quantities of these OP pesticides residues in soil, drinking water, and food, which are treats to public health and safety [6,7]. Rapid and accurate detection of these pesticides on food and in the environment is great importance for public health reasons. Up to date, there are a lot of efforts that has been devoted to develop a methodology for detecting Mal and Glyp, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8,9], gas chromatography (GC) [10,11], and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [12,13]. These methods are sensitive for the accurate measurements of these pesticides but have some weaknesses that limit their use in on-site monitoring, such as the need for time-consuming sample processing, as well as experienced operators and expensive chemical reagents. As a result, the development of a cost-effective, simple, fast, and precise method of Mal and Glyp detection has become increasingly significant.

Over the last two decades, chemosensors have been brought more attention to detect biological molecules, anions, metal ions, and pesticides due to their some advantages such as low cost, fast response, high selectivity, high sensitivity, and on-site use over the other methods [14–16]. So far, several optical (colorimetric and fluorescence) sensors have been developed to determine OP pesticides in environment and on food [17–20]. The overall responses of these sensors to pesticides are generally based on nanoparticle aggregation [21,22], enzyme inhibition [23–26], and paper strips [27]. These sensors have some limitations. The nanoparticles are easily affected by pH and environmental conditions, thus limiting their real sample applications. Also, uncontrolled aggregation of nanoparticles causes low selectivity [28]. Paper-based sensors also suffer from instability, long optimization time, long production time, and temperature sensitivity [29]. On the other hand, enzyme inhibition responses are collected indirectly, and require time and equipment [30]. Most recently, we have been focused on developing better sensors for pesticides. We reported four sensors that allowed us detect Glyp in real samples via a reaction-based system [31] with high selectivity in low concentration. However, the reaction between the sensors and the pesticide is little bit time consuming (completed 5–7 min.). Hence, simple, rapid, highly selective, and sensitive optical sensors for pesticides are needed. For this purpose, we combined 4-(dimethylamino)cinnamaldehyde with 2-aminopyrrazine to synthesize the sensor, CNP. The colorimetric and optical properties of CNP were examined by UV–vis, fluorescence spectroscopy and the naked eye in the presence of various metal ions and pesticides. CNP have a fast and remarkable selectivity for Glyp and Mal with low detection limits.

Parathion (Par) was bought from Dr. Ehrenstorfer. 2-aminoprazine (98 %), 4-(dimethylamino)cinnamaldehyde (98 %), Malathion (Mal), Glyphosate (Glyp), and Fenamiphos (Fena) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH (Steinheim, Germany). Unless otherwise noted, other chemicals and solvents used for experiments were purchased, and they were used without further purification.

A Bruker Ultrashield Plus Biospin Avance III 400 MHz NaNoBay FT NMR was used to collect NMR spectra (1H and 13C) at ambient temperature (298 K). UV–vis spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer at 298 K. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-7100 spectrophotometer at 298 K. An Ohaus-starter 2100 pH meter was used for pH measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the sensor, CNP
ce:section.ce:para: 4-(Dimethylamino)cinnamaldehyde (0.175 g, 1 mmol) and 2-aminopyrazine (0.095 g, 1 mmol) were dissolved in ethanol (1 mL) and stirred with reflux at 80 °C for 24 h. The mixture was allowed to cool to the ambient temperature and the precipitated solid was filtered off. The resulting solid was crystallized in hot ethanol and red colored sensor, CNP, was obtained (0.200 g Yield 79 %). 1H NMR (400.2 MHz, CDCl3): δ (ppm) 8.90 (d, J = 9.4 Hz, 1H), 8.50 (s, 1H), 8.29 (s, 2H), 7.39 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 2H), 7.20 (d, J = 15.5 Hz, 1H), 6.92 (d, J = 25.0 Hz, 1H), 6.61 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 2H), 2.96 (s, 6H). 13C NMR (101.6 MHz, CDCl3): δ (ppm) 165.78, 156.13, 150.85, 148.17, 141.93, 141.56, 140.66, 129.47, 128.79, 122.06, 110.92, 39.09. MALDI-TOF: C15H16N4 [M]+ calculated 252.137 found 252.935.

Stock solutions (10 mM) of the metal ions (NaCl, CaCl2, AlCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, AgNO3, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, HgCl2, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 3CdSO4·8H2O, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl3·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O) were prepared in distilled water. The stock solutions (1 mM) of CNP were prepared in ACN, and then diluted to 20 μM in ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v). Gly (1.70 mg) was dissolved in distilled water to provide the stock solution with the concentration of 10 mM. On the other hand, those of Mal (10 mM) were prepared in methanol. In the spectroscopic studies (UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission), for UV–vis and fluorescence experiments, the volume of solutions was taken as 1.5 mL and 3.0 mL, respectively. The absorption spectra of CNP with and without pesticides or metal ions were measured in the range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra (at 497 nm or 595 nm) were collected under the excitation of 400 nm with both excitation and emission slit widths that are 10 nm. The spectra were collected at ca. 5 min upon addition of each selected pesticides and metal ions to the sensor solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the pesticides (Glyp, Mal) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the capacity of CNP to detect the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) in real samples, the sensor was used to detect Glyp in soil, tap water, and potato samples, to detect Mal in apple and tap water samples. The potato and apple sample were bought from a local market. The tap water samples and soil samples collected from a corn crop field were obtained in Karaman (Turkey). For apple samples, apple samples were washed with distilled water and cut into small pieces. 10 g of apple samples was taken and extracted in 10 mL of diethyl ether for 2 h. It was filtered through a 0.45 mm microporous membrane. The solvent was evaporated and the residue dissolved in 2 mL of ethanol and it was then diluted with the working solvent system [32]. For soil samples, 20 g of soil sample was weighed and dispersed in 20 mL of ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v) for 30 min under ultrasonication [19]. It was filtered first with filter paper, then with a 0.45 µm and 0.22 µm microporous membrane, respectively. The soil filtrate was further diluted 4 times with ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v). For potato samples, 20 g of potatoes were ground in a mixer and centrifuged for 10 min at 5000 rpm. After filtering through a 0.45 μm membrane, potato juice was diluted with ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v) [33]. Various concentrations of Glyp or Mal (1.0, 3.0 and 5.0 ppm) were added to the real samples and examined with CNP under the identical conditions recommended for the selectivity studies.

The sensor CNP was successfully synthesized by combining a cinnamaldehyde moiety and a 2-aminopyrazine moiety in a one-step procedure as presented in Scheme 1 . The sensor was characterized using NMR (1H and 13C) and mass (MALDI-TOF) spectroscopy (Figs. S1-S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic studies of the sensor, CNP
ce:section.ce:para: There are four nitrogen atoms in the compound CNP, which is a Schiff base derivative. Therefore, it has the potential to respond to hydrogen ions as well. First of all, the behavior of the sensor CNP towards hydrogen ions was studied. CNP was hydrolyzed at pH = 1.0. It responded to hydrogen ions in the pH 2–3 range. CNP was yellow in the pH = 4–10 range and neutral in this range (Fig. S4). Selectivity tests for CNP were carried out in the pH 4–10 range. First, the selectivity studies were conducted in only acetonitrile (ACN) solvent; however, the sensor gave response to several pesticides and metal ions selected, and the color of the sensor solution turned light yellow from orange-yellow color (Fig. S5). In organic solvents, metal ions such as Fe3+, Al3+ can lower the pH of the solvent [34] and these pH changes could affect the sensor selectivity. Therefore, using a buffered solution could resist to pH changes caused by Fe3+ and Al3+.

Color change experiments were then carried out in ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v). As expected, the color of CNP solution did not change when Al3+ and Fe3+ were added. On the other hand, the addition of Glyp, Mal, and Cu2+ caused a change in color of CNP solution (Fig. S6). The spectroscopic properties of CNP were then investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers under same condition as the naked-eye detection experiment. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, CNP itself exhibited an absorption peak around 410 nm which may be assigned to π-π* charge transfer transition [35]. When the pesticides (Fena, Mal, Par, and Glyp) and the metal ions (Cu2+ , Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Na+, Cd2+, and Al3+) were added to the solution of CNP, Glyp and Cu2+ caused a shift in the absorption spectrum of CNP from 410 nm to 393 nm. The addition of Mal resulted in a decrease in the absorbance of CNP. The emission spectra of CNP were collected after excitation at 400 nm wavelength. CNP showed a strong fluorescence emission at 595 nm (Fig. 1b). When Mal was added to the solution of CNP, the fluorescence intensity of CNP decreased. On the other hand, when Glyp and Cu2+ were added, the fluorescence at 595 nm decreased, while a new peak was formed at 490 nm, causing a shift of 105 nm (Fig. 1b). As the wavelengths were shifted to smaller wavelengths in both absorbance and fluorescence measurements, the change in color was observed from orangish-yellow to light yellow in the sensor solution (Fig. S6). Other pesticides and metal ions tested did not cause changes in both the absorption and fluorescence spectrum of CNP.

In pesticide/metal ion selectivity, a sensor must be selective for a specific target. There may be a number of other cations and pesticides in the environment where the target is located. So, it could potentially interfere with the sensor's response. The effect of the pesticides and the metal ions selected on Glyp and Mal selectivity was investigated. We decided that fluorescence spectroscopy was the most suitable option, and the fluorescence intensities of CNP + Glyp/Mal at 595 nm and 497 nm were collected with the other metal ions and pesticides selected. Thus, the pesticides and metals tested were first added to CNP solutions, and Glyp and/or Mal were then added to each of them. As shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S7, Mal detection is not possible in the presence of Glyp at both 595 nm and 497 nm. However, in the absence of Glyp, Mal can be detected. On the other hand, Glyp can be detected in the presence of Mal at 497 nm (Fig. S7).

After it was determined that the sensor CNP was selective against Glyp and Mal, UV–vis and fluorescence titration experiments were performed to further examine the interaction between CNP and the pesticides (Glyp, Mal). For this purpose, increasing amounts of the pesticides were added separately to CNP (100 µM) in ACN/Acetate buffer (5 mM pH = 5.6, 1:1 v/v) and UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were collected. As depicted in Fig. S8a, with the increase in Glyp concentration, the absorbance peak intensity at 410 nm decreased while the absorbance intensity at 393 nm increased. This increase continued linearly until approximately 1.0 equivalent of Glyp was added, and then it became plateaued. These results suggested that the binding stoichiometry between CNP and Glyp was 1:1. Fig. 3 a presented the fluorescence spectra of CNP with the increase of Glyp concentration. The fluorescence intensity at 595 nm decreased with the increase of the concentration of Glyp, and this decrease continued until 1.0 equivalent of Glyp was added, and then no change was observed in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3a). This finding also supports the 1:1 stoichiometry between Glyp and CNP. Similar experiments were carried out with Mal. As seen in Fig. S8b and Fig. 3b, the absorbance intensity at 410 nm and the fluorescence intensity at 595 nm decreased with the increase in Mal concentration, and these decreases continued until approximately 1.0 equivalent Mal was added, and then the absorbance and fluorescence intensities did not change, meaning that the binding stoichiometry between CNP and Mal is 1:1. Job's plot experiments were also utilized to estimate the binding stoichiometry of CNP-Glyp and CNP-Mal [36]. The results supported the 1:1 binding ratio between CNP and the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) (Fig. S9 and Fig S10).

Mass spectrometry was also used to better identify the binding ratio between CNP and the pesticides (Glyp and Mal). The positive mass spectrum of CNP/Glyp solution (Fig. S11) showed a peak at m/z = 423.294, which is assignable to [CNP-Glyp]+ (calculated, m/z = 422.159). For Mal, upon the adding of Mal to the sensor (1 μM each in ACN), an intensive peak was observed at m/z = 410.100 (Fig. S12). It is not an expected result. However, it may be assigned to [CNP-Mal]+ with losing the diethylsuccinate moiety as explained in the literature [37,38]. These results also suggested that the stoichiometry between the sensor and the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) is 1:1.

NMR titration experiments (1H, and 31P) were performed to eludicate the sensing mechanism between the sensor and the pesticides (Glyp and Mal). We first carried out the NMR experiments for Glyp by adding Glyp (dissolved in d-acetone) to d-acetone solution of CNP. Fig. 4 presents the 1H NMR spectra of CNP and CNP + Glyp. Some protons on the sensor were numbered (see inset in Fig. 4). We followed the shifts in these protons. The doublet signal at 8.91–8.89 ppm assigned to H -CN (Ha) group slightly shifted to 9.03–9.00 ppm after the addition of Glyp. The protons (Hb, Hc, and Hd) in the pyrazine ring of the sensor showed peaks at 8.29 ppm and 8.50 ppm. After the interaction with Glyp, while the peak at 8.29 ppm downshifted to 8.44 ppm and 8.42 ppm as doublets, the peak at 8.50 (Hd) ppm did not show any shift. We also collected the 31P NMR of Glyp and CNP + Glyp. As depicted in Fig. S13, the phosphorus peak of Glyp at 5.80 ppm did not change after the interaction with the sensor. It can be concluded that the phosphorus atom on Glyp is not related to the binding with the sensor. Glyp may be interacted with the sensor through hydrogen bonds [39–41]. The proposed binding mechanism was presented in Scheme 2 .

Fig. 5 shows the NMR spectra of CNP and CNP + Mal in d-acetone. We also followed the changes in the protons numbered in the sensor's structure. The interaction between CNP and Mal did not affect the hydrogen of the azomethine group H-CN (Ha) at 8.91–8.89 ppm. The peak at 8.50 ppm (Hd) disseapperaed and a new peak at 8.41 ppm emerged. 31P NMR spectra were investigated to better understand the binding mechanism. As seen in Fig. S14, the peak at 95.69 ppm shifted to 54.47 ppm after the addition of CNP. When five coordinate P(V) goes to six coordinate P(V) compounds, up-field shift is observed. The five coordinate P(V) is a Lewis acid, and behaves as a s-acceptor towards the Lewis acidic anion. The electron density on phosphorus increases, and it results in an up-field shift in the 31P NMR spectrum [42]. Collectively, Mal may bind to CNP with its P atom as proposed in Scheme 3 .

We also investigated the binding between CNP and Cu2+. Fluorescence titration experiments and Job’s method were used to determine the binding stoichiometry, and the results (Fig. S15) showed that the binding stoichiometry was 1:1. The proposed binding mechanism between CNP and Cu2+ was presented in Scheme S1.

The detection limits for the pesticides (Mal and Glyp) were calculated using the data collected from the UV–vis titration experiments. As depicted in Figs. S16 and S17, the linear equations were; y = 0.0036x (µM) + 1.8561 (R2 = 0.95) in the range of 0–100 µM for Glyp with a detection limit of 0.375 µM, and y = -0.01324x (µM) + 2.9170 (R2 = 0.95) in the range of 0–100 µM for Mal with a detection limit of 0.066 µM. The detection limits were calculated from 3σ/k, where k is the slope of the calibration curves, and σ is standard deviation of the sensor (n = 10) [43]. Both of the detection limits calculated are lower than the maximum concentrations permitted for Glyp and Mal in drinking water [44,45].

Detection limits for Glyp and Mal were also determined from fluorescence data. The fluorescence based detection limits were calculated to be 2.0 μM for Glyp and 3.98 μM for Mal (Fig. S18 and Fig. S19), which were higher than the values calculated from absorbance data.

As depicted in Fig. 6 , the detection limit with the naked eye for Glyp was also carried out. The yellow color of CNP started to change with the addition of 4 µM of Glyp. But, complete color change was noticed when 8 µM of Glyp was added. In the solvent system studied, the detection limit for Glyp with the naked eye is 8 µM. The naked eye detection limit studies for Mal were not presented because the color changes for Mal could not be fully distinguished in the working medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the sensor, CNP, in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water, apple, potatoes, and soil were chosen as real samples to confirm the applicability of the sensor, CNP. The real samples were prepared using the methods described in the experimental section. The equations on the Figures (Fig. S16 and Fig. S17) were used to calculate the concentrations of Glyp and Mal. The results collected for the pesticides (Glyp and Mal) are shown in Table 1 and the results show that the sensor can be used in practical application for measuring Glyp and Mal in real sample.

In this study, we developed a novel sensor, CNP, for the detection of the pesticides, Glyp and Mal. The sensor was easily prepared by combining 4-(dimethylamino)cinnamaldehyde with 2-aminopyrazine. CNP gave response to Glyp via H-bonding and to Mal via the coordination. The interactions between CNP and the pesticides caused color changes, which ranged from yellow to light-yellow for Glyp and yellow to pale-yellow for Mal. The sensor also exhibited excellent selective absorbance/fluorescence spectral changes for Glyp and Mal among the pesticides and metal ions selected. Moreover, it is possible to separately detect Mal and Glyp with fluorescence responses collected at 497 nm. The detection limits for Glyp and Mal were determined (0.375 μM for Glyp and 0.066 μM for Mal) from UV–vis measurements, and both of the detection limits calculated are lower than the maximum concentrations permitted for Glyp and Mal in drinking water. For the practical application, the sensor was successfully applied to monitor the pesticides in real samples; malathion in apple and tap water samples, and glyphosate in potato, tap water and soil samples. All these results suggest that CNP is an excellent sensor for Glyp and Mal.

Ziya Aydin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mukaddes Keskinateş: Methodology, Investigation. Şeyma Akın: Methodology, Investigation. Hülya Keleş: Methodology, Investigation. Mustafa Keleş: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK, Project number: 219Z289) for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114340.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
